section_text_parts: In OAuth2 a "scope" is just a string that declares a specific permission required.
It doesn't matter if it has other characters like : or if it is a URL.
Those details are implementation specific.
For OAuth2 they are just strings.
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
In OAuth2 a "scope" is just a string that declares a specific permission required.

It doesn't matter if it has other characters like : or if it is a URL.

Those details are implementation specific.

For OAuth2 they are just strings.

section_text_parts: The OAuth2 spec actually requires a field grant_type with a fixed value of password , but OAuth2PasswordRequestForm doesn't enforce it.
If you need to enforce it, use OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict instead of OAuth2PasswordRequestForm .
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
The OAuth2 spec actually requires a field grant_type with a fixed value of password , but OAuth2PasswordRequestForm doesn't enforce it.

If you need to enforce it, use OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict instead of OAuth2PasswordRequestForm .

section_text_parts: The OAuth2PasswordRequestForm is not a special class for FastAPI as is OAuth2PasswordBearer .
OAuth2PasswordBearer makes FastAPI know that it is a security scheme. So it is added that way to OpenAPI.
But OAuth2PasswordRequestForm is just a class dependency that you could have written yourself, or you could have declared Form parameters directly.
But as it's a common use case, it is provided by FastAPI directly, just to make it easier.
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
The OAuth2PasswordRequestForm is not a special class for FastAPI as is OAuth2PasswordBearer .

OAuth2PasswordBearer makes FastAPI know that it is a security scheme. So it is added that way to OpenAPI.

But OAuth2PasswordRequestForm is just a class dependency that you could have written yourself, or you could have declared Form parameters directly.

But as it's a common use case, it is provided by FastAPI directly, just to make it easier.

section_text_parts: The instance of the dependency class OAuth2PasswordRequestForm won't have an attribute scope with the long string separated by spaces, instead, it will have a scopes attribute with the actual list of strings for each scope sent.
We are not using scopes in this example, but the functionality is there if you need it.
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
The instance of the dependency class OAuth2PasswordRequestForm won't have an attribute scope with the long string separated by spaces, instead, it will have a scopes attribute with the actual list of strings for each scope sent.

We are not using scopes in this example, but the functionality is there if you need it.

section_text_parts: For a more complete explanation of **user_dict check back in the documentation for Extra Models .
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text: For a more complete explanation of **user_dict check back in the documentation for Extra Models .

section_text_parts: In the next chapter, you will see a real secure implementation, with password hashing and JWT tokens.
But for now, let's focus on the specific details we need.
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
In the next chapter, you will see a real secure implementation, with password hashing and JWT tokens.

But for now, let's focus on the specific details we need.

section_text_parts: By the spec, you should return a JSON with an access_token and a token_type , the same as in this example.
This is something that you have to do yourself in your code, and make sure you use those JSON keys.
It's almost the only thing that you have to remember to do correctly yourself, to be compliant with the specifications.
For the rest, FastAPI handles it for you.
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
By the spec, you should return a JSON with an access_token and a token_type , the same as in this example.

This is something that you have to do yourself in your code, and make sure you use those JSON keys.

It's almost the only thing that you have to remember to do correctly yourself, to be compliant with the specifications.

For the rest, FastAPI handles it for you.

section_text_parts: The additional header WWW-Authenticate with value Bearer we are returning here is also part of the spec.
Any HTTP (error) status code 401 "UNAUTHORIZED" is supposed to also return a WWW-Authenticate header.
In the case of bearer tokens (our case), the value of that header should be Bearer .
You can actually skip that extra header and it would still work.
But it's provided here to be compliant with the specifications.
Also, there might be tools that expect and use it (now or in the future) and that might be useful for you or your users, now or in the future.
That's the benefit of standards...
tutorial/security/simple-oauth2::section.section_text:
The additional header WWW-Authenticate with value Bearer we are returning here is also part of the spec.

Any HTTP (error) status code 401 "UNAUTHORIZED" is supposed to also return a WWW-Authenticate header.

In the case of bearer tokens (our case), the value of that header should be Bearer .

You can actually skip that extra header and it would still work.

But it's provided here to be compliant with the specifications.

Also, there might be tools that expect and use it (now or in the future) and that might be useful for you or your users, now or in the future.

That's the benefit of standards...
